channel.title: NYT > Movies
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: At Cannes, the Specter of Hollywood Always Looms Large (Even in Its Absence)
item.description: The lack of big studio movies is apparent in the festival’s quiet start, though the filmmakers James Gray and Jane Schoenbrun have made an impression.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis
item.media:description: On hand for the “Fast and the Furious” tribute at Cannes were, from left, Meadow Walker (Paul Walker’s daughter), Jordana Brewster, Vin Diesel, Michelle Rodriguez and Neil H. Moritz.

item.title: ‘The Wizard of the Kremlin’ Review: The New Rasputin
item.description: Paul Dano and Jude Law star in a movie about the rise of Vladimir Putin and a fictional version of his right-hand man.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Is God Is’ Review: The Fires This Time
item.description: Kara Young and Mallori Johnson play twins bent on revenge in the playwright Aleshea Harris’s powerhouse film debut.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: How Meryl Streep Delivers Criticism in ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’
item.description: The director David Frankel narrates a comedic scene featuring Streep and Anne Hathaway.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: ‘Obsession’ Review: I Love You to Death
item.description: Passion becomes possession in Curry Barker’s supernatural, be-careful-what-you-wish-for horror movie.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:description: Inde Navarrette, left, with Michael Johnston in “Obsession.”

item.title: 6 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: At Cannes 2026, Gaza Becomes a Political Flashpoint
item.description: The event’s leaders immediately jumped into the debate over whether festival participants should speak out on issues like Israel and Gaza.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: From left, the Cannes Film Festival president, Iris Knobloch, the jury president, Peter Jackson, and the festival director, Thierry Frémaux.

item.title: Five Free Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s collection, including Richard Linklater’s “Before” trilogy, heralds the blooming of love, and all the turmoil that comes with it.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: Watching ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ as an Elegy for Magazines
item.description: The film may be about fashion, but for some editors and writers, it gets a lot right about the dire state of fashion journalism.
item.dc:creator: Clarissa Cruz
item.media:description: Meryl Streep, left, and Anne Hathaway as fashion magazine editors dealing with a crumbling media landscape in “The Devil Wears Prada 2.”

item.title: Five Action Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include brawling teenagers, vengeful widowers and retired swordsmen.
item.dc:creator: Robert Daniels

item.title: ‘In the Grey’ Review: Cavill and Gyllenhaal Bring the Ruckus
item.description: Guy Ritchie’s latest is a sleek, sun-drenched actioner in which morality is fuzzy but the fashion is sharp.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: Martin Short and the Secret to Finding Joy While Surviving Tragedy
item.description: The comedy star, who is the subject of a new documentary, has faced a series of unimaginable losses. Yet he says there are always laughs to remember.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman and Thea Traff
item.media:description: Grieving gives you “muscles to survive,” Martin Short said.

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include an animated critter voiced by Michael B. Jordan and a nature documentary about the orangutans of Southeast Asia.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: The SpongeBob Movie: Search for SquarePants (Movie)

item.title: ‘Been Here Stay Here’ Documentary Examines Tangier Island
item.description: “Been Here Stay Here” examines the complicated Chesapeake Bay dynamic when many residents don’t believe in man-made climate change.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ | Anatomy of a Scene
item.description: The director David Frankel narrates a sequence from his film starring Meryl Streep and Anne Hathaway.
item.dc:creator: Mekado Murphy

item.title: ‘Magic Hour’ Review: Grief Encounters
item.description: Katie Aselton’s melodrama follows a woman in the California desert who is reeling after a mysterious marriage conflict.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘Life Hack’ Review: A Heist That Never Leaves the Screen
item.description: A group of teenagers seem to be targeting a cryptocurrency billionaire out of boredom, but they have a more sympathetic motive in this hacker film.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘Decorado’ Review: Cute Mice Meet Cold Orwellian Despair
item.description: This surreal satire blends anthropomorphic charm with existential dread to critique capitalism, though its meta-commentary leaves some haunting questions frustratingly unanswered.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips

item.title: ‘Agatha’s Almanac’ Review: Living Off the Land
item.description: Over six years, a filmmaker captured her aunt’s largely self-sufficient lifestyle and eccentricities on a 54-acre farm in Canada.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: Ellen Burstyn on Her Favorite Books and Her Love of Poetry
item.description: “Of course, at 93 I am not as good at memorizing as I used to be,” the Oscar-winning actress and author says. “But it is good exercise.”
item.category: Poetry Says It Better: Poems to Help You Wake Up (Book)

item.title: Koji Suzuki, Whose Horror Novels Inspired ‘Ring’ Movies, Dies at 68
item.description: Sometimes called the Stephen King of Japan, he helped create a genre known as J-horror and spawned one of the highest-grossing horror films ever made.
item.dc:creator: Jeré Longman
item.media:credit: Jennifer S. Altman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Koji Suzuki in 2004. Although his horror fiction made him wealthy, he was not a fan of the genre. “I really dislike most horror writing,” he told The New York Times.

item.title: Mel Brooks Donates His Archives to the National Comedy Center
item.description: The comedian is contributing some 20,000 documents and photos covering his career, including his time in the Army and the lyrics for “Springtime for Hitler.”
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: ‘A New Leaf’: A Romantic Comedy, With Murder on the Mind
item.description: This 1971 movie, restored and revived for a week at the IFC Center, offers ample evidence of Elaine May’s screwball genius.
item.dc:creator: J. Hoberman

item.title: ‘The A List’ Review: The Diaspora, Described
item.description: This documentary spotlights the individual histories and struggles of 15 figures from all walks of life, connected by their inclusion in the Asian and Pacific diasporas.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu
item.category: The A List: 15 Stories From Asian and Pacific Diasporas (Movie)

item.title: How Naomi Ackie Learned to Act Without Fear
item.description: She’s had breakout moments before. But with “I Love Boosters,” she’s moving beyond hype.
item.dc:creator: Olivia Ovenden

item.title: Claire Maurier, the Narcissistic Mother in ‘400 Blows,’ Dies at 97
item.description: A landmark of New Wave cinema, “The 400 Blows” brought her enduring global attention. She later appeared in “La Cage Aux Folles” and “Amélie.”
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Claire Maurier as a bistro owner in “Amélie.” A whimsical comedy-drama-romance, it was a major commercial success in France and one of the highest-grossing French films ever released in the United States.

item.title: Officers Sue Affleck and Damon’s Company Over ‘The Rip’ Portrayal
item.description: Two Miami law enforcement officials say the Netflix crime drama uses real-life details and portrays them in a negative light.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin

item.title: ‘Paranormal Activity’ to Haunt Broadway This Summer
item.description: Inspired by the popular film series, the play has already run in Britain and in four U.S. cities. It arrives in New York in August after a Boston stop.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Paranormal Activity: A New Story Live on Broadway (Play)
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: The play, billed as a modern ghost story, had its first American stop in Chicago last fall.

item.title: Rex Reed, Film Critic Known for Acerbic Reviews, Dies at 87
item.description: He fawned over Old Hollywood stars and sparred with Frank Sinatra. Nora Ephron marveled at his ability to get his subjects to say the things they did.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:credit: Vincent Tullo for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rex Reed in 2017. If he disliked someone or — worse — found the person merely uninteresting, it was wise to duck and cover.

item.title: At Netflix Is a Joke, the Attention Economy Rules the Festival
item.description: Though stand-up by stars like John Mulaney and Dave Chappelle was on the program, roasts, podcasts and other shows that incorporated comedy were hot.
item.dc:creator: Jason Zinoman

item.title: In ‘Should I Marry a Murderer’ and ‘Trust Me,’ Informants Risk Almost Everything
item.description: In two new Netflix docuseries, women put their lives, safety and sanity on the line to help law enforcement bring down the bad guy.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam
item.category: Should I Marry a Murderer (TV Program)
Trust Me: The False Prophet (TV Program)
Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
item.media:description: Christine Marie, who spent years collecting evidence to help bring down a child sex predator, in the docuseries “Trust Me: The False Prophet.”

item.title: A Reporter Shares His Secrets to Covering the Cannes Film Festival
item.description: Kyle Buchanan, who has attended the annual launchpad for Oscar contenders in France since 2013, subsists on Red Bull and around four hours of sleep.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr
item.media:description: The red carpet at the 2025 Cannes Film Festival. Many movies screened during the festival go on to earn Academy Award nominations.

item.title: Kevin Hart Roast: Highlights From Tom Brady, the Rock, Katt Williams and More
item.description: Tom Brady, the Rock, Chelsea Handler and even Hart’s longtime rival Katt Williams told jokes at Hart’s expense at a Netflix roast.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:description: Actor and comedian Kevin Hart sat through many jokes.

item.title: Cannes Film Festival Preview: 4 Story Lines to Watch for
item.description: The lineup isn’t very starry, but changes in the Oscar rules could affect the Palme d’Or choice. That’s one development we’re tracking.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan
item.media:description: Pedro Almodóvar at Cannes in 2023. He’s returning with “Bitter Christmas.”

item.title: Lost Movie Music? On CD? La-La Land Is an Anomaly. (And a Success.)
item.description: The California record label scours movie studio archives and composers’ personal collections to locate music from older films or TV recordings.
item.dc:creator: Brian Raftery
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Verboys (center), one of the founders of La-La Land Records, at work at the label’s offices in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: ‘The Lost Boys’ Helped Define the ’80s in More Ways Than One
item.description: The film that’s been adapted into a new musical created a memorable aesthetic with its cast and outfits. Its ideas about the nuclear family also made it a movie for its time.
item.dc:creator: Maya Phillips
item.media:description: From left, Billy Wirth, Kiefer Sutherland, Brooke McCarter and Alex Winter in “The Lost Boys.”

item.title: Rocky Has Entered the Building
item.description: The statue has long drawn fans to the steps of the Philadelphia Museum of Art, but now it’s inside, anchoring an exhibition that investigates race, activism and violence.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Hawthorne

item.title: Hugh Jackman in a Truly PG-Rated Murder Mystery
item.description: Our movie critic Alissa Wilkinson reviews “The Sheep Detectives” and asks: Why aren’t more movies like it?
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson, Gabriel Blanco, Edward Vega, Haimy Assefa and Laura Salaberry
